validation_rules: require every drift row to cite source signal, source ref, repair route, validation ref, and public runtime target ref
reject source authority claims, live repair authority, source mutation, automatic doctrine promotion, private runtime data export, provider payload export, and release authority
require public runtime receipt authority, body-free public fixture rows, body-import verification, and secret-exclusion scan

require exported source-module bodies to be represented by source_open_body_imports with body_material_count, body_material_ids, material_classes, source_manifest_refs, aggregate_floor_ref, and body-text exclusion flags
receipt_expectations: receipt must cite row counts, selected pattern ids, negative-case coverage, secret-exclusion scan, body-import verification, source-open body import summary, target refs, authority ceiling, and anti-claim
failure_modes: world-model drift rows cite source refs but no copied non-secret source-module bodies are present in an exported bundle
copied world-model or view-quality source-module digest diverges from the macro source digest
copied source-module manifest omits the world-model reducer, /api/drift endpoint, view-quality action-map builder, or focused action-map regression test
copied source-module anchors fail to prove the drift_aggregate_v1, /api/drift, or view_quality_action_map_v1 mechanisms are present
source-module bodies are duplicated into receipts instead of remaining bundle files with receipt-level paths, hashes, counts, and anchor evidence
selected pattern ids drift from the validated drift row ids
projection-drift rows authorize live repair, source mutation, automatic doctrine promotion, provider payload export, private runtime export, or release
macro projection import accounting treats source refs or runtime receipts as body imports without exact target files and digest verification
anti_claim: This standard governs public body-free projection-drift runtime receipts and target refs. It does not authorize source authority, live repairs, source mutation, doctrine promotion, provider calls, private runtime export, or release.
